text: I would like to check in this suitcase, and I have one {{c1::carry-on bag}} to bring into the cabin.
explanation: A carry-on bag (or hand luggage) is the small suitcase or backpack that passengers are permitted to bring onto the plane with them rather than checking it.
usage:
Pattern: carry-on bag / carry-on luggage
  • Make sure your carry-on bag fits in the overhead bin.
  • I only travel with a carry-on bag to save time.
spanish: Me gustaría registrar esta maleta, y tengo una maleta de mano para llevar en la cabina.

scenario: English Phonetics: Airport Gate Connected Speech ✈️
text: At the gate, the announcement "We will begin boarding in ten minutes" blends the last two words via consonant-to-consonant linking, making "in ten" sound like {{c1::"int-ten" [ɪnˈtɛn]}}.
explanation: When a word ends in /n/ and the next begins with /t/, the alveolar nasal /n/ assimilates or links directly to the alveolar stop /t/ to allow smooth, rapid articulation, resulting in a single elongated nasal-to-stop transition.
usage:
Phonetic Drill: in ten minutes → "in-ten" [ɪnˈtɛn]
Contrastive Practice: Pronounce both 'n' and 't' as a single continuous block without a glottal pause.
spanish: En la puerta, el anuncio "We will begin boarding in ten minutes" fusiona las últimas dos palabras mediante enlace consonante-consonante, haciendo que "in ten" suene como "int-ten".

scenario: English Daily Life: Airport Baggage Claim ✈️
text: At the baggage claim desk, if your luggage is missing, you should report it politely saying: "Excuse me, my bag {{c1::didn't come out}} on the carousel, and I'd like to {{c2::file a claim}}."
explanation: To "file a claim" is the official terminology for registering a lost item report. Saying "didn't come out" is the natural phrasal expression for baggage that did not arrive.
usage:
Pattern: file a claim / didn't come out on [carousel]
  • I need to file a claim for my damaged baggage.
  • My suitcase didn't come out on carousel 4.
spanish: En el mostrador de reclamo de equipaje, si tu maleta se perdió, debes reportarlo diciendo educadamente: "Excuse me, my bag didn't come out on the carousel, and I'd like to file a claim."

text: Could you please tell me where the {{c1::baggage drop-off}} counter is?
explanation: The 'baggage drop-off' is the designated counter where passengers who have already checked in online can drop off their luggage before heading to security.
usage:
Pattern: baggage drop-off / bag drop
  • Where is the bag drop for airline passengers?
  • You can bypass the main line if you only need the baggage drop-off.
spanish: ¿Podría decirme por favor dónde está el mostrador de entrega de equipaje?

text: I need to check in my luggage, but I want to keep this briefcase as my {{c1::personal item}}.
explanation: A 'personal item' is a small bag (like a purse, laptop bag, or small backpack) that must fit under the seat in front of you on a plane, distinct from standard carry-on luggage.
usage:
Pattern: personal item
  • Most budget airlines allow one personal item for free.
  • Your laptop bag counts as a personal item.
spanish: Necesito registrar mi equipaje, pero quiero conservar este maletín como mi artículo personal.

text: The flight has been delayed, so we need to wait at the {{c1::departure gate}}.
explanation: The 'departure gate' (or boarding gate) is the specific area in an airport terminal where passengers assemble to board their aircraft.
usage:
Pattern: departure gate / boarding gate
  • Our departure gate has changed from gate 12 to gate 15.
  • Please report to the departure gate immediately.
spanish: El vuelo se ha retrasado, así que tenemos que esperar en la puerta de salida/embarque.

text: Excuse me, does this flight have a {{c1::layover}} in New York?
explanation: A 'layover' (or connection) is a period of waiting between flights at an intermediate airport before reaching the final destination.
usage:
Pattern: layover / connection
  • We have a three-hour layover in Chicago.
  • I prefer direct flights because I hate long layovers.
spanish: Disculpe, ¿este vuelo tiene una escala en Nueva York?

text: We need to go through {{c1::security screening}} before we can access the duty-free area.
explanation: The 'security screening' is the checkpoint in an airport where passengers and their baggage are inspected by security officers and scanning machines.
usage:
Pattern: security screening / security check
  • The line at the security screening was incredibly long.
  • Remove all laptops and liquids before security screening.
spanish: Necesitamos pasar por el control de seguridad antes de poder acceder al área libre de impuestos.

text: Please have your {{c1::boarding pass}} and ID ready for inspection.
explanation: A 'boarding pass' is a document provided by an airline during check-in, giving a passenger permission to board the airplane for a specific flight.
usage:
Pattern: boarding pass / boarding document
  • You can show your digital boarding pass on your phone.
  • Please print your boarding pass at the kiosk.
spanish: Por favor, tenga su pase de abordar e identificación listos para inspección.

text: My flight was overbooked, and they offered me travel vouchers if I {{c1::volunteered to take}} a later flight.
explanation: To 'volunteer to take' a later flight means agreeing to give up your seat on an overbooked flight in exchange for compensation (such as travel vouchers or money).
usage:
Pattern: volunteer to take [flight]
  • No one volunteered to take the next flight, so they started bumped passengers.
  • I volunteered to take a later flight because the vouchers were worth $500.
spanish: Mi vuelo estaba sobrevendido, y me ofrecieron vales de viaje si me ofrecía como voluntario para tomar un vuelo posterior.

text: Could you tell me which terminal is for {{c1::domestic flights}}?
explanation: 'Domestic flights' are flights that stay within the boundaries of the same country, as opposed to international flights.
usage:
Pattern: domestic flights / domestic terminal
  • All domestic flights depart from Terminal 2.
  • I am traveling within the country, so I need to check in for domestic flights.
spanish: ¿Podría decirme qué terminal es para vuelos nacionales?

text: I missed my flight; is there a desk where I can {{c1::rebook my ticket}}?
explanation: To 'rebook a ticket' means to change the reservation on a ticket, typically after missing a flight or if a flight is canceled, allocating the passenger to a new flight.
usage:
Pattern: rebook my ticket / rebook a flight
  • The airline helped me rebook my ticket for the next available flight.
  • Can I rebook my ticket online without paying a fee?
spanish: Perdí mi vuelo; ¿hay algún mostrador donde pueda volver a reservar mi boleto?

text: Please place all metal objects in the bin before going through the {{c1::metal detector}}.
explanation: A 'metal detector' is the electronic security portal in an airport checkpoint that scans passengers for metallic weapons or devices.
usage:
Pattern: metal detector / walk-through scanner
  • Step through the metal detector one at a time.
  • My belt buckle triggered the metal detector.
spanish: Por favor, coloque todos los objetos metálicos en la bandeja antes de pasar por el detector de metales.

text: We need to clear {{c1::customs control}} before exiting the airport.
explanation: To clear 'customs control' means passing through the government checkpoint at an international border to declare goods and verify compliance with imports.
usage:
Pattern: customs control / clear customs
  • It took us an hour to clear customs control in London.
  • Do you have anything to declare at customs control?
spanish: Necesitamos pasar el control de aduanas antes de salir del aeropuerto.

text: Excuse me, where is the office for {{c1::lost and found}}?
explanation: The 'lost and found' (or lost property office) is the department in a public facility where lost items are kept for owners to claim.
usage:
Pattern: lost and found / lost property
  • I left my umbrella on the plane; I should check the lost and found.
  • The lost and found office is near the baggage claim.
spanish: Disculpe, ¿dónde está la oficina de objetos perdidos y encontrados?

text: The captain announced that we are preparing for {{c1::takeoff}}.
explanation: 'Takeoff' is the moment or process when an aircraft leaves the ground and rises into the air.
usage:
Pattern: takeoff / prepare for takeoff
  • Please fasten your seatbelts in preparation for takeoff.
  • The takeoff was very smooth despite the rain.
spanish: El capitán anunció que nos estamos preparando para el despegue.

text: Please remain seated with your seatbelt fastened until the plane has finished {{c1::landing}}.
explanation: 'Landing' is the process of bringing an aircraft down to the ground or runway at the end of a flight.
usage:
Pattern: landing / smooth landing
  • The pilot made a safe landing despite the crosswinds.
  • Remain seated until the landing is complete.
spanish: Por favor, permanezca sentado con su cinturón abrochado hasta que el avión haya terminado de aterrizar.

text: Is there a currency exchange booth in the {{c1::arrivals hall}}?
explanation: The 'arrivals hall' is the public area in an airport terminal where passengers emerge after clearing customs and baggage claim.
usage:
Pattern: arrivals hall / departures hall
  • My family was waiting for me in the arrivals hall.
  • You can buy a local SIM card in the arrivals hall.
spanish: ¿Hay una cabina de cambio de divisas en la sala de llegadas?

text: Passengers are allowed to bring one piece of checked luggage and one {{c1::cabin bag}}.
explanation: A 'cabin bag' (or hand luggage) is a small suitcase that complies with airline size rules to be stored in the overhead bins.
usage:
Pattern: cabin bag / hand luggage
  • Make sure your cabin bag is not too heavy.
  • I only traveled with a cabin bag to avoid baggage claim.
spanish: Los pasajeros pueden llevar una pieza de equipaje registrado y una maleta de cabina.

text: If your bags are overweight, you will have to pay an {{c1::excess baggage fee}}.
explanation: An 'excess baggage fee' is a charge made by an airline for luggage that exceeds the weight or size allowance.
usage:
Pattern: excess baggage fee / overweight luggage fee
  • They charged me a $50 excess baggage fee because my suitcase was 5kg over.
  • Avoid the excess baggage fee by wearing your heavy coat.
spanish: Si sus maletas tienen sobrepeso, tendrá que pagar una tarifa por exceso de equipaje.
